text: The surgeon demonstrates the sutured closure of the renal capsule, carefully placing sutures to approximate the edges of the capsule.

The surgeon uses two O-vicryl sutures on a CT-one needle, cut to six inches, to close the renal capsule. Each suture is secured with a knot and a hemlock clip. The suture is passed through the renal capsule, and the hemlock clip is anchored on the capsule. The suture is then passed through the opposite side of the defect. The assistant applies a large hemlock clip in the middle of the suture, and the console surgeon uses the sliding hemlock clip technique to approximate the capsular edges and close the defect. Additional sutures are placed in an interrupted fashion to close the defect and distribute tension, with the option for a running closure.

The surgeon holds the suture perpendicular to the renal capsule while sliding down the clip to prevent tearing through the renal parenchyma. The upper pole defect is closed using similar techniques, with the most challenging stitches placed first. The initial suture is placed left-handed and back-handed, facilitating easier clip placement by the assistant. Previously placed capsular sutures are used to retract the kidney, optimizing exposure for subsequent needle throws.
The surgeon unclamps the renal hilum following the reconstruction of the renal defect, allowing blood flow to resume to the kidney.
